news_summary: Scientists have made breakthroughs in superconductivity and quantum technology, while NASA advances firefighter safety with new sensors, and researchers uncover the secret behind gold's lasting shine.
news_summary_fr: Les scientifiques ont réalisé des avancées majeures dans les domaines de la supraconductivité et de la technologie quantique, tandis que la NASA améliore la sécurité des pompiers grâce à de nouveaux capteurs et que des chercheurs dévoilent le secret de l'éclat durable de l'or.
news_summary_es: Los científicos han logrado avances decisivos en el campo de la superconductividad y la tecnología cuántica, mientras que la NASA mejora la seguridad de los bomberos con nuevos sensores y los investigadores desvelan el secreto que se esconde tras el brillo duradero del oro.

articles.title: Scientists break 30-year superconductivity record at normal pressure
articles.summary: Scientists at the University of Houston have shattered a long-standing superconductivity record, creating a material that can conduct electricity with zero resistance at the highest temperature ever achieved under normal pressure conditions. Their breakthrough pushes superconductivity to 151 Kelvin (minus 122°C), beating a record that stood for more than 30 years.
articles.content:
University of Houston researchers have achieved a major superconductivity breakthrough by setting a new temperature record for superconductors operating under ambient pressure conditions. The advance could eventually help create more efficient electrical grids, improved energy storage systems, faster electronics, and new technologies for fusion energy and medical imaging.

Scientists from the Texas Center for Superconductivity (TcSUH) and the University of Houston department of physics reached a superconducting transition temperature (Tc) of 151 Kelvin (about minus 122 degrees Celsius). That is now the highest Tc ever reported for a superconductor functioning at ambient pressure since superconductivity was first discovered in 1911.

The transition temperature marks the point where a material can carry electricity with zero resistance. Increasing this temperature has been one of the biggest goals in superconductivity research because higher operating temperatures could make superconducting technologies far more practical and affordable.

The findings by physicists Ching-Wu Chu and Liangzi Deng were published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences. Funding for the work came from Intellectual Ventures, the state of Texas through TcSUH, and several foundations.

"Transmitting electricity in the grid loses about 8% of the electricity," said Chu, professor of physics, TcSUH founding director and the paper's senior author. "If we conserve that energy, that's billions of dollars of savings and it also saves us lots of effort and reduces environmental impacts."

Why Superconductors Matter

Superconductors are materials that allow electricity to flow without resistance. Because no energy is lost as heat, they could dramatically improve the efficiency of electrical systems. Scientists also see superconductors as critical for technologies such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), fusion reactors, quantum technologies, and ultrafast electronics.

The challenge is that most superconductors only work at extremely low temperatures, requiring expensive cooling systems that limit widespread use.

"Once we bring the material to ambient pressure, it becomes much more accessible for scientists to use well-developed instrumentation to investigate it and further develop technologies for ambient condition operations," said Deng, assistant professor of physics, principal investigator at the TcSUH and lead author of the paper.

New Record Breaks Decades-Old Barrier

Researchers have spent decades searching for superconducting materials with increasingly higher transition temperatures.

A major milestone came in 1987 when Chu and his collaborators discovered that a material known as YBCO could become superconducting at minus 180 degrees C, or 93 K. That discovery helped launch a global race to develop high-temperature superconductors.

In 1993, scientists discovered a mercury-based copper-oxide ceramic called Hg1223 that reached superconductivity at minus 140 degrees C, or 133 K. That material held the ambient-pressure record for more than 30 years.

The new University of Houston achievement pushes the record 18 degrees C higher to 151 K.

Pressure Quenching Creates Stable Superconductivity

The breakthrough relied on a process known as pressure quenching. While pressure techniques are commonly used in other fields, including diamond production, the method is relatively new in superconductivity research.

Researchers first subjected the material to extremely high pressure, which enhanced its superconducting behavior and increased its transition temperature. While still under pressure, the material was cooled to a carefully chosen temperature before the pressure was suddenly removed.

That rapid release effectively preserved the enhanced superconducting properties, allowing the material to remain stable even after returning to normal pressure conditions.

"Other researchers have shown that reaching superconductivity at room temperature under pressure is achievable," Chu said. "Our method shows that it is possible to retain that state without maintaining pressure."

A Step Toward Room-Temperature Superconductors

Although room-temperature superconductivity at ambient pressure remains out of reach, researchers say the new record is an important advance toward that goal. Room temperature is roughly 300 K, leaving a gap of about 140 degrees C from the newly achieved record.

"This finding has great potential," Chu said. "We believe, with enough people working on it and given enough time, we should be able to realize the potential."

Chu and Deng also contributed to a companion perspective paper funded by Intellectual Ventures and published in PNAS. The paper discusses six different approaches researchers could use to raise superconducting temperatures further, including pressure quenching.

"Room-temperature superconductivity has been seen as a 'holy grail' by scientists for over a century," said Rohit Prasankumar, director of superconductivity research at Intellectual Ventures. "The UH team's result shows that this goal is closer than ever before. However, the distance between the new record set in this study and room temperature is still about 140 degrees C. Closing this gap will require concerted, intentional efforts by the broader scientific community, including materials scientists, chemists, and engineers, as well as physicists."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a major scientific breakthrough in superconductivity at ambient pressure, setting a new temperature record that has stood for over 30 years. This advance has broad implications for energy efficiency, electrical grids, medical imaging, and future technologies, representing a significant step toward practical room-temperature superconductors with large-scale societal benefits. The article provides detailed context about the discovery, its significance, and future potential.
articles.personality_title: Scientists set new record for superconductivity at normal pressure
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Superconductors are special materials that let electricity flow without losing any energy. Usually, they only work at very cold temperatures, which makes them hard to use in everyday life. For over 30 years, the highest temperature for superconductors working at normal pressure was stuck at minus 140°C.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Houston created a new superconductor that works at 151 Kelvin (about minus 122°C) without needing extra pressure. They used a method called pressure quenching, where they first squeezed the material under high pressure, cooled it down, then quickly released the pressure. This kept the material’s superconducting abilities stable at normal pressure.

**Impact** – This is the highest temperature ever recorded for a superconductor working without extra pressure. It means superconductors could become easier and cheaper to use in things like electrical grids, which now lose about 8% of electricity as heat. It could also improve medical machines like MRI scanners and help develop new technologies such as fusion energy.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to keep raising the temperature at which superconductors work so they can eventually operate at room temperature (around 300 K). This new record brings them closer, but there is still a gap of about 140 degrees Celsius to close. Researchers plan to explore new materials and methods, hoping to develop practical superconductors that work without expensive cooling.

**One-sentence takeaway** – University of Houston scientists have raised the temperature record for superconductors at normal pressure, moving closer to making energy-saving technologies more practical and affordable.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques battent un record de supraconductivité à pression normale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les supraconducteurs sont des matériaux spéciaux qui laissent passer l’électricité sans aucune perte d’énergie. En général, ils ne fonctionnent qu’à des températures très basses, ce qui complique leur utilisation au quotidien. Depuis plus de 30 ans, la température la plus élevée pour des supraconducteurs à pression normale était bloquée à -140°C.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Houston ont créé un nouveau supraconducteur qui fonctionne à 151 Kelvin (environ -122°C) sans pression supplémentaire. Ils ont utilisé une méthode appelée trempe sous pression, où ils ont d’abord soumis le matériau à une forte pression, l’ont refroidi, puis ont relâché rapidement la pression. Cela a permis de garder les propriétés supraconductrices stables à pression normale.

**Impact** – C’est la température la plus élevée jamais enregistrée pour un supraconducteur fonctionnant sans pression supplémentaire. Cela signifie que les supraconducteurs pourraient devenir plus faciles et moins coûteux à utiliser dans des systèmes comme les réseaux électriques, qui perdent actuellement environ 8% de l’électricité sous forme de chaleur. Cela pourrait aussi améliorer les machines médicales comme les IRM et aider à développer de nouvelles technologies comme l’énergie de fusion.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent continuer à augmenter la température de fonctionnement des supraconducteurs pour qu’ils puissent un jour fonctionner à température ambiante (environ 300 K). Ce nouveau record rapproche cet objectif, mais il reste un écart d’environ 140 degrés Celsius à combler. Les chercheurs prévoient d’explorer de nouveaux matériaux et méthodes pour développer des supraconducteurs pratiques sans refroidissement coûteux.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques de l’Université de Houston ont battu le record de température pour les supraconducteurs à pression normale, rapprochant les technologies économes en énergie d’une utilisation plus pratique et abordable.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos rompen récord de superconductividad a presión normal
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los superconductores son materiales especiales que permiten que la electricidad fluya sin perder energía. Normalmente, solo funcionan a temperaturas muy bajas, lo que dificulta su uso diario. Durante más de 30 años, la temperatura más alta para superconductores a presión normal se mantuvo en -140°C.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Houston crearon un nuevo superconductor que funciona a 151 Kelvin (unos -122°C) sin necesitar presión extra. Usaron un método llamado temple de presión, donde primero presionaron mucho el material, lo enfriaron, y luego liberaron la presión rápidamente. Esto mantuvo las propiedades superconductoras estables a presión normal.

**Impacto** – Esta es la temperatura más alta registrada para un superconductor que funciona sin presión extra. Esto significa que los superconductores podrían ser más fáciles y baratos de usar en cosas como las redes eléctricas, que ahora pierden alrededor del 8% de la electricidad en forma de calor. También podría mejorar máquinas médicas como los escáneres MRI y ayudar a desarrollar nuevas tecnologías como la energía de fusión.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren seguir aumentando la temperatura a la que funcionan los superconductores para que algún día operen a temperatura ambiente (alrededor de 300 K). Este nuevo récord acerca esa meta, pero aún queda una diferencia de unos 140 grados Celsius por cerrar. Los investigadores planean explorar nuevos materiales y métodos para crear superconductores prácticos sin necesidad de costosos sistemas de enfriamiento.

**Frase clave** – Científicos de la Universidad de Houston han elevado el récord de temperatura para superconductores a presión normal, acercando tecnologías que ahorran energía a un uso más práctico y accesible.
articles.image_prompt: A glowing crystalline lattice structure radiating vibrant blue and icy white hues, suspended above an open book of scientific formulas, with gentle swirling currents of warm light symbolizing breakthrough energy flowing seamlessly through the lattice, set against a soft gradient background of twilight purples and deep blues.

articles.title: NASA Develops Sensor to Improve Firefighter Safety
articles.summary: With peak wildfire season approaching, scientists with NASA’s FireSense project have created low-cost thermal sensors to install on fire bulldozers that will alert firefighters when heat from a nearby fire reaches a dangerous level. The sensors also provide researchers with important data on what happens beneath the canopy during a fire. In April, researchers and […]
articles.content:
Alabama Forestry Commission wildland firefighter Jason Berry teaches NASA Wildland Fires Technology Program Manager Teresa Kauffman how to drive a fire bulldozer during a stakeholder event April 23-24 in Andalusia, Alabama. NASA FireSense scientists have been working with the AFC to integrate thermal sensors onto these dozers, which notify the dozer operator if the radiant heat from a nearby fire reaches a dangerous threshold. NASA/Milan Loiacono

With peak wildfire season approaching, scientists with NASA’s FireSense project have created low-cost thermal sensors to install on fire bulldozers that will alert firefighters when heat from a nearby fire reaches a dangerous level. The sensors also provide researchers with important data on what happens beneath the canopy during a fire.

In April, researchers and firefighters gathered in southern Alabama to discuss challenges and advances in firefighting, and to demonstrate the new technology. The event was part of a collaboration between NASA and the Alabama Forestry Commission (AFC). The goal: to make firefighting safer and gather critical data on fire behavior.

“As we try to develop technologies that allow us to understand and respond to wildfires with our partners, ground observations are vital to provide context for what we are seeing from space,” said Ian Brosnan, program manager for wildland fires at NASA’s Ames Research Center in California’s Silicon Valley.

To view this video please enable JavaScript, and consider upgrading to a web browser that

supports HTML5 video The Alabama Forestry Commission tests the new thermal sensor developed by NASA’s FireSense project for their fleet of fire dozers, during the initial integration in September 2025. After FireSense scientists installed the sensor, AFC operators drove the dozer next to a test fire, at the distance the dozers normally operate on a fire line. The thermal sensors performed as planned and have since been deployed on active wildfires. NASA/Ryan Wade

Dozers on the fire line

Firefighters nationwide use bulldozers, colloquially referred to as fire dozers, on the front line of a fire to clear vegetation and to create fire breaks, which slow or stop a wildfire’s spread. This often puts dozers and their operators within feet of the flames.

The AFC is switching its fleet to a model of bulldozer that has an enclosed cab called an “envirocab.” While envirocabs are safer for operators than open cabs, the enclosure makes it more difficult to gauge when radiant heat from the fire has reached a dangerous temperature.

Alabama Forestry Commission fire analyst Ethan Barrett gives an overview of fire dozer operations to scientists and researchers from NASA’s FireSense project and other university and commercial partners during the April event. NASA/Milan Loiacono

“It’s not so much about what’s going to burn the tractor up as what’s going to shut the tractor down,” said Ethan Barrett, AFC fire analyst. The electrical wiring can short or even melt from high heat, stranding the operator in a dangerous environment.

That’s where NASA comes in. According to Brosnan, developing thermal sensors for the AFC was an opportunity to create technology that has immediate impact on firefighter safety, while also providing scientists with valuable information about what happens on the ground during a fire.

It’s not so much about what’s going to burn the tractor up as what’s going to shut the tractor down. Ethan barrett AFC Fire Analyst

How sensors work

The AFC’s requirements for a sensor were simple: it needed to be low-cost and easy to operate.

“We used commercial, off-the-shelf components to make this,” said Jennifer Fowler, science integration manager for the wildland fires program at NASA’s Langley Research Center in Hampton, Virginia. “The thermocouple that sits in the window to measure temperature, for example, is the same one used in an oven or a kiln.”

Jennifer Fowler, NASA Wildland Fires science integration manager (left) and Ryan Wade, research scientist with the University of Alabama, Huntsville and NASA FireSense (right) hold a version of the low-cost thermal sensor they developed to install on fire dozers. The sensor uses an off-the-shelf thermocouple, found in ovens and kilns, to read the radiant heat coming in from a nearby fire. When it reaches an unsafe temperature, the sensor triggers a blinking LED light on the dashboard (right), signaling the operator to move away from the fire line. NASA/Milan Loiacono

That thermocouple is wired to a simple LED light attached to the dashboard that’s directly in the operator’s line of sight. When the thermocouple senses an unsafe temperature, the LED starts blinking. The whole system is powered by AA batteries.

“While installing the second sensor, we realized we needed an extra piece, so we just ran out to the local hardware store to grab it,” said Ryan Wade, research scientist with the University of Alabama, Huntsville and NASA FireSense. “NASA’s expertise in this case comes not in the novelty of the instrument itself, but in figuring out how to solve the problem quickly and integrate that technology into their existing system.”

Fowler installed the first of these sensors in September 2025, and Wade installed the second in March 2026.

“Since their installation, we have run them on wildfires and prescribed burns and they’ve been effective,” Barrett said. “They work exactly as intended, and the operators have said it leads to better situational awareness. Based on the success of this pilot, we are looking at outfitting all the dozers in our fleet.”

Driving fire science forward

Co-developing these thermal sensors is the latest milestone in a relationship the two agencies have been building for more than a year. NASA scientists led training classes on weather and soil moisture with the AFC last spring and worked with AFC ground crews to test airborne instruments on active wildfires.

Moving forward, NASA FireSense and the AFC are planning to integrate the Fire Thermal InfraRed Spectrometer, or FireTIRS, which will measure temperature, spread rate, flame length, fire convection, and gas emissions.

James Thompson, an assistant research professor at University of Texas at Austin and a principal investigator with NASA’s Earth Science Technology Office, tests out locations on a fire dozer where the FireTIRS thermal infrared imager could be mounted. Thompson was part of a stakeholder event held between NASA’s FireSense project and the Alabama Forestry Commission (AFC), which included demonstrating thermal sensors on the AFC’s fire dozers.

Fowler is also evaluating anemometers and compact cameras for the dozers. Anemometers provide data on wind speed and direction, while compact cameras provide data on burn severity, rate of spread, and the type, volume, and consumption of fuels.

The data this suite of instruments can gather would fill an important gap in creating a well-rounded understanding of fire.

“This is the dataset that will get us to the next generation of fire models,” Fowler said. “It gives us the detailed understanding we need to create tools that can give firefighters more advanced notice of what a fire will do. On a wildfire, that extra time is everything.”

To view more photos from the FireSense campaign visit: nasa.gov/firesense
articles.reasoning: The article describes NASA's development of low-cost thermal sensors that improve firefighter safety by alerting operators of dangerous heat levels on fire bulldozers. This technology has a broad real-world impact by enhancing safety for firefighters nationwide during wildfire season. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a meaningful collaboration between NASA and the Alabama Forestry Commission, with tangible benefits and plans for further technological integration.
articles.personality_title: NASA creates heat sensors to protect firefighters on the front lines
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Wildfires are dangerous and often require firefighters to use bulldozers, called fire dozers, to clear plants and stop fires from spreading. These dozers get very close to flames, putting operators at risk, especially because newer dozers have enclosed cabs that make it hard to feel when heat becomes dangerous.

**What happened** – NASA’s FireSense project developed low-cost thermal sensors that attach to fire dozers. These sensors measure the heat from nearby fires and alert the operator with a blinking light if the temperature is too high. In April 2026, NASA and the Alabama Forestry Commission (AFC) tested this technology together during a special event in Alabama. The sensors have already been used successfully on real fires.

**Impact** – This sensor helps protect firefighters by giving them a clear warning before the heat damages the dozer’s electrical parts or puts them in danger. It also gives scientists important information about fire behavior under the forest canopy, which can improve how fires are managed. The sensors are easy to use and affordable because they use common parts found in ovens and kilns.

**What’s next step** – NASA and the AFC plan to install these sensors on all the fire dozers in Alabama. They are also working on adding more tools, like thermal infrared cameras and wind sensors, to better understand fires and predict their movement. This could help firefighters get earlier warnings and make safer decisions.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s new heat sensors on fire bulldozers help protect firefighters by warning them of dangerous temperatures while also improving fire science for better wildfire response.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA crée des capteurs de chaleur pour protéger les pompiers en première ligne
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les feux de forêt sont dangereux et nécessitent souvent l’utilisation de bulldozers appelés "fire dozers" pour dégager la végétation et stopper la propagation des flammes. Ces engins s’approchent très près du feu, ce qui met en danger leurs opérateurs, surtout avec les nouvelles cabines fermées qui rendent difficile la détection de la chaleur dangereuse.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le projet FireSense de la NASA a développé des capteurs thermiques peu coûteux à installer sur ces bulldozers. Ces capteurs mesurent la chaleur du feu proche et avertissent l’opérateur par une lumière clignotante si la température devient trop élevée. En avril 2026, la NASA et la Commission forestière de l’Alabama (AFC) ont testé cette technologie lors d’un événement spécial en Alabama. Les capteurs ont déjà été utilisés avec succès sur de vrais incendies.

**Impact** – Ce capteur aide à protéger les pompiers en leur donnant un avertissement clair avant que la chaleur n’endommage les composants électriques du bulldozer ou ne mette leur sécurité en danger. Il fournit aussi aux scientifiques des données importantes sur le comportement du feu sous la canopée, ce qui améliore la gestion des incendies. Les capteurs sont faciles à utiliser et abordables car ils utilisent des pièces courantes trouvées dans les fours et les fours à poterie.

**Prochaine étape** – La NASA et l’AFC prévoient d’équiper tous les bulldozers de l’Alabama de ces capteurs. Ils travaillent aussi à ajouter d’autres outils, comme des caméras infrarouges thermiques et des capteurs de vent, pour mieux comprendre les incendies et prédire leur évolution. Cela pourrait aider les pompiers à recevoir des avertissements plus tôt et à prendre des décisions plus sûres.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les nouveaux capteurs de chaleur de la NASA sur les bulldozers protègent les pompiers en les alertant des températures dangereuses tout en améliorant la science du feu pour une meilleure réponse aux incendies de forêt.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA crea sensores de calor para proteger a los bomberos en primera línea
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los incendios forestales son peligrosos y a menudo requieren que los bomberos usen bulldozers, llamados "fire dozers", para limpiar la vegetación y detener la propagación del fuego. Estos bulldozers se acercan mucho a las llamas, lo que pone en riesgo a sus operadores, especialmente porque los modelos nuevos tienen cabinas cerradas que dificultan sentir cuándo el calor es peligroso.

**Qué pasó** – El proyecto FireSense de la NASA desarrolló sensores térmicos de bajo costo para instalar en los fire dozers. Estos sensores miden el calor del fuego cercano y alertan al operador con una luz intermitente si la temperatura es demasiado alta. En abril de 2026, la NASA y la Comisión Forestal de Alabama (AFC) probaron esta tecnología juntos en un evento especial en Alabama. Los sensores ya se han usado con éxito en incendios reales.

**Impacto** – Este sensor ayuda a proteger a los bomberos al darles una advertencia clara antes de que el calor dañe las partes eléctricas del bulldozer o ponga en peligro al operador. También proporciona a los científicos información importante sobre cómo se comporta el fuego bajo el dosel del bosque, lo que puede mejorar el manejo de incendios. Los sensores son fáciles de usar y económicos porque usan piezas comunes que se encuentran en hornos y kilns.

**Próximos pasos** – La NASA y la AFC planean instalar estos sensores en todos los fire dozers de Alabama. También están trabajando en agregar más herramientas, como cámaras infrarrojas térmicas y sensores de viento, para entender mejor los incendios y predecir su movimiento. Esto podría ayudar a los bomberos a recibir advertencias más tempranas y tomar decisiones más seguras.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los nuevos sensores de calor de la NASA en bulldozers de incendios ayudan a proteger a los bomberos al alertarlos sobre temperaturas peligrosas y mejoran la ciencia del fuego para una mejor respuesta a incendios forestales.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a sturdy fire bulldozer with an enclosed cab, its dashboard softly glowing with a blinking LED light, positioned near stylized, gently billowing flames that emit subtle thermal waves; nearby, abstract silhouettes of firefighters stand attentively, while a glowing, simplified thermal sensor device is visibly mounted on the bulldozer, all set against a calm forest backdrop with muted natural colors.

articles.title: First quantum grandfather clock could probe where gravity comes from
articles.summary: Researchers have designed a quantum version of a pendulum clock. It could shed light on timekeeping in the quantum realm
articles.content:
Pendulum clocks were much more accurate than those that came before Panumas Nikhomkhai / Alamy

The first complete design for a quantum grandfather clock uses a single atom, tiny mirrors and light. Building it could help our understanding of what makes any clock accurate in the quantum realm and explore ideas at the edge of physics.

At the most rudimentary level, time can be measured with something simple, like sand trickling through an hourglass. But timekeeping became a lot more accurate once mechanical clocks, like the grandfather or pendulum clock, were invented in the 17th century. Matteo Brunelli at Collège de France and his colleagues have now shown that such clocks have a quantum equivalent.

“We asked ourselves the question: ‘Can a pendulum clock work according to the laws of quantum mechanics?’ We couldn’t be sure,” he says.

Each pendulum clock has three basic elements, starting with the pendulum that defines the clock’s ticks with its swings. Next are the weights within the clock that leverage gravity’s downward pull to make the pendulum move. Finally, a pendulum clock requires an “escapement mechanism”, which converts the pendulum’s swings into the motion of the clock’s arms and provides the pendulum with little kicks of energy to prevent friction from slowing it down. Specifically, for the pendulum to keep swinging left-to-right by the same amount every time, the escapement mechanism must control the up-and-down motion of the weights.

The researchers developed a mathematical model that replicated all these features with quantum objects. In their design, the clock is a cavity comprising two mirrors that face each other – one is fixed and the other can oscillate back-and-forth. Between the mirrors sits an atom that can have three different energies. Tiny temperature fluctuations in the cavity’s environment make the atom transition from one energy to another, and some transitions are accompanied by the atom emitting a photon. This photon bounces between the mirrors, making one of them oscillate, analogous to falling weights setting the pendulum into motion.

The atom plays the role of the escapement mechanism, repeatedly moving through its energy states, ensuring a sequence of ticks and tocks. Brunelli says that this is the smallest an escapement mechanism can possibly be. The team’s mathematical analysis showed that if everything was tuned correctly, the quantum clock would settle into stable, reliable, ticking behavior – just like a pendulum clock should.

Brunelli says that unlike the world’s best atom-based clocks that need to be controlled by lasers, this clock would be autonomous, operating more like a self-standing thermodynamic machine. Autonomous quantum clocks have been designed before, but because they didn’t maintain the same even oscillations through the escapement mechanism, they were less accurate, he says.

In fact, the new clock broke an accuracy limit called the “thermodynamic uncertainty relation” that constrained many past autonomous clocks. This is because any clock’s accuracy relates to how much effort it would take to make it run backwards – and the new clock’s accuracy was proportional to its irreversibility in the way thought to be favourable for particularly good timekeeping.

Sreenath Manikandan at the Tata Institute of Fundamental Research Hyderabad in India says that understanding autonomous clocks is critical for understanding timekeeping because they do not rely on another clock to remain accurate, so they capture the most elementary version of the process. And the more we understand quantum clocks at the most elementary level, the more useful they can be for probing new physics, such as how gravity behaves in the quantum realm, he says. “A deep understanding of the working mechanisms of a clock is highly desirable, and I think that the new work presents a major progress in this direction,” says Manikandan.

Experiments with tiny cavities and photons are common, so many of the ingredients that would be necessary for building the new clock in the lab already exist. However, Brunelli says that the escapement mechanism is novel enough to make building such an experiment technically challenging. “But it’s not completely unreasonable,” he says.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a novel quantum grandfather clock design that could significantly advance understanding of timekeeping in the quantum realm and probe fundamental physics questions, such as the nature of gravity. This represents a meaningful scientific breakthrough with broad implications for physics and technology, supported by detailed explanation of the design and its potential impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists design first quantum grandfather clock to explore time and gravity
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Traditional pendulum clocks use a swinging weight to measure time accurately. These clocks, invented in the 17th century, improved timekeeping by using gravity and mechanical parts. Now, scientists are trying to create a similar clock that works using the strange rules of quantum mechanics, which govern very small particles like atoms.

**What happened** – Researchers led by Matteo Brunelli at Collège de France designed a complete model of a quantum grandfather clock. Instead of a swinging pendulum and weights, the clock uses a single atom, tiny mirrors facing each other, and light particles called photons. The atom changes energy levels, releasing photons that bounce between the mirrors and cause one mirror to move back and forth. This movement acts like the pendulum, creating regular “ticks.” The atom also acts like the clock’s mechanism that keeps it swinging steadily.

**Impact** – This quantum clock design is the smallest possible version of a pendulum clock and works on its own without needing lasers or external control. It overcomes limits that stopped earlier autonomous quantum clocks from being very accurate. Because it keeps time so well in the quantum world, scientists believe it can help study deep questions about how time and gravity work at a tiny scale, where normal physics doesn’t always apply.

**What’s next step** – Building the clock in a laboratory will be challenging because of its new, delicate parts. However, many of the pieces like tiny cavities and photons have already been used in experiments. If successful, this clock could open new ways to test ideas about gravity and time using quantum technology.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have created the first full design of a tiny quantum grandfather clock that could improve timekeeping and help explore how gravity works at the smallest scales.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques conçoivent la première horloge à pendule quantique pour étudier le temps et la gravité
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les horloges à pendule traditionnelles utilisent un poids oscillant pour mesurer le temps avec précision. Inventées au XVIIe siècle, elles ont amélioré la mesure du temps grâce à la gravité et à des pièces mécaniques. Aujourd’hui, des chercheurs tentent de créer une horloge similaire fonctionnant selon les règles étranges de la mécanique quantique, qui gouverne les très petites particules comme les atomes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe menée par Matteo Brunelli au Collège de France a conçu un modèle complet d’horloge à pendule quantique. Au lieu d’un pendule et de poids, cette horloge utilise un seul atome, de petites miroirs face à face, et des photons, des particules de lumière. L’atome change d’état d’énergie et émet des photons qui rebondissent entre les miroirs, faisant bouger l’un d’eux. Ce mouvement joue le rôle du pendule, créant des « tics » réguliers. L’atome agit aussi comme le mécanisme qui maintient ce mouvement stable.

**Impact** – Ce design d’horloge quantique est la plus petite version possible d’une horloge à pendule et fonctionne de manière autonome, sans lasers ni contrôle externe. Il dépasse les limites qui empêchaient les horloges quantiques autonomes précédentes d’être très précises. Grâce à cette précision, les scientifiques pensent qu’elle peut aider à étudier en profondeur comment le temps et la gravité fonctionnent à une très petite échelle, où la physique classique ne suffit plus.

**Prochaine étape** – Construire cette horloge en laboratoire sera difficile à cause de ses pièces délicates et nouvelles. Mais beaucoup des éléments, comme les cavités minuscules et les photons, ont déjà été utilisés en laboratoire. Si elle est construite avec succès, cette horloge pourrait ouvrir de nouvelles façons de tester des idées sur la gravité et le temps grâce à la technologie quantique.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont conçu la première horloge à pendule quantique complète, capable d’améliorer la mesure du temps et d’aider à comprendre la gravité aux plus petites échelles.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos diseñan el primer reloj de abuelo cuántico para explorar el tiempo y la gravedad
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los relojes de péndulo tradicionales usan un peso que se mueve para medir el tiempo con precisión. Inventados en el siglo XVII, mejoraron la medición del tiempo usando la gravedad y partes mecánicas. Ahora, científicos intentan crear un reloj similar que funcione con las extrañas reglas de la mecánica cuántica, que gobierna partículas muy pequeñas como los átomos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores liderados por Matteo Brunelli en el Collège de France diseñaron un modelo completo de un reloj de abuelo cuántico. En lugar de un péndulo y pesos, el reloj usa un solo átomo, espejos diminutos enfrentados y partículas de luz llamadas fotones. El átomo cambia de nivel de energía y emite fotones que rebotan entre los espejos, haciendo que uno se mueva. Este movimiento actúa como el péndulo, creando “tics” regulares. El átomo también funciona como el mecanismo que mantiene el movimiento estable.

**Impacto** – Este diseño es la versión más pequeña posible de un reloj de péndulo y funciona solo, sin necesidad de láseres o control externo. Supera los límites que impedían que otros relojes cuánticos autónomos fueran muy precisos. Debido a su buena precisión en el mundo cuántico, los científicos creen que puede ayudar a estudiar preguntas profundas sobre cómo funcionan el tiempo y la gravedad a escalas muy pequeñas, donde la física normal no siempre aplica.

**Próximo paso** – Construir el reloj en un laboratorio será difícil por sus partes nuevas y delicadas. Sin embargo, muchos de los componentes, como cavidades pequeñas y fotones, ya se usan en experimentos. Si se logra construir, este reloj podría abrir nuevas formas de probar ideas sobre la gravedad y el tiempo usando tecnología cuántica.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos han creado el primer diseño completo de un reloj de abuelo cuántico que podría mejorar la medición del tiempo y ayudar a explorar cómo funciona la gravedad en las escalas más pequeñas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a classic grandfather clock transformed into a delicate quantum device: an elegant wooden clock frame enclosing a glowing atom suspended between two softly oscillating mirrors, with gentle beams of light bouncing inside the cavity, symbolizing the quantum escapement mechanism driving the rhythmic swings of a pendulum made of light and energy.

articles.title: We may finally know why gold stays so shiny
articles.summary: Gold is chemically inert and so doesn't tarnish, but exactly why had been a mystery
articles.content:
Gold is chemically inert and so doesn't tarnish, but exactly why had been a mystery

Gold doesn’t tarnish like other metals mauritius images GmbH/Alamy

Silver goes dull, copper turns green and iron rusts, but gold always stays shiny. Why this is the case has remained a mystery, but researchers may have finally figured out what makes the valuable metal so resistant to change and how to tarnish it.

Gold is chemically inert, meaning that it doesn’t react with molecules from its surroundings, such as oxygen in the air. This is great news for jewelry, but it limits gold’s usefulness in chemistry, where researchers think it could be a useful catalyst – if only it could be nudged out of its inertness.

Matthew Montemore and Santu Biswas at Tulane University in Louisiana investigated a phenomenon called reconstruction, which happens when a piece of gold is cut, creating a new surface.

“The atoms just hate being on a surface so much that they completely rearrange,” says Montemore. Often, they rearrange into a pattern resembling repeating hexagons, then don’t shuffle further because their energy is low in this arrangement. Reconstruction isn’t common among metals, so the researchers wondered whether it contributes to gold’s inertness.

They used a supercomputer to simulate the quantum states of atoms for several different rearrangements that can happen during reconstruction and to analyse their interactions with oxygen. For a reconstructed gold surface to lose some of its luster, a molecule of oxygen would have to first split in two upon hitting it. The researchers’ simulations revealed that such splitting requires a lot of energy for atoms in a hexagonal pattern, which makes tarnishing very unlikely, but a lot less energy when their arrangement is rectangular.

Because the hexagonal pattern is more common, gold tends to stay shiny. Biswas says that this connection between atoms’ geometry, reconstruction and oxidation has never been considered before.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Lost in Space-Time Untangle mind-bending physics, maths and the weirdness of reality with our monthly, special-guest-written newsletter. Sign up to newsletter

Understanding it now could help researchers make gold more useful as a catalyst, says Hongliang Xin at Virginia Tech. “The exciting takeaway is that gold’s catalytic behaviour may be tuned by controlling surface reconstruction,” he says. Montemore says that one way to control reconstruction, like nudging atoms into rectangular patterns that are less inert to oxygen, could be by placing a piece of gold in an electrical circuit and applying a voltage.

“[This work] is telling us something that perhaps hadn’t been considered before. Definitely there’s something here for experimentalists to go and look at,” says Andrew Beale at University College London. He says that the idea of using gold as a catalyst has already been proven for certain reactions by using nano-sized particles of the precious metal. The project of making gold useful in this new way is then rather realistic, but questions remain about how the team’s analysis can be connected to objects like such nanoparticles, which tend to have curved surfaces, says Beale.

Going forward, the researchers want to extend their analysis to reactions with molecules other than oxygen, as well as gold alloys in addition to pure gold.
articles.reasoning: This article reports a scientific breakthrough in understanding why gold remains shiny due to its atomic surface reconstruction, which has significant implications for improving gold's catalytic use in chemistry. The discovery has broad potential impact in materials science and catalysis, offering a new way to tune gold's chemical properties, which could benefit various industries and research fields. The article provides sufficient detail about the research methods, findings, and future directions, making it a substantive and inspiring advancement.
articles.personality_title: Scientists uncover why gold stays shiny and how to change it
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Gold is a metal that never tarnishes like silver or iron, but scientists did not fully understand why it keeps its shiny look so well. This has limited how gold can be used, especially in chemistry.

**What happened** – Researchers at Tulane University studied how the surface atoms of gold rearrange when the metal is cut. They found that gold atoms form a hexagonal pattern on the surface, which makes it very hard for oxygen to react and cause tarnishing. Using supercomputers, they showed that this pattern needs a lot of energy to change, which keeps gold shiny most of the time.

**Impact** – This discovery explains a long-standing mystery about gold’s shine by linking it to the shape of atoms on its surface. It also opens up new possibilities for changing gold’s surface to make it react more, which could help scientists use gold as a catalyst to speed up chemical reactions. This could affect many areas, from making better electronics to improving industrial processes.

**What's next step** – The researchers want to test how other molecules react with gold and look at gold alloys, which are mixtures with other metals. They also want to explore ways to control the surface pattern by using electricity, which might allow gold to be used in new ways.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have discovered that gold’s shiny look comes from a special atomic pattern on its surface, and changing this pattern could make gold useful for new chemical reactions.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent pourquoi l’or reste brillant et comment le modifier
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’or est un métal qui ne ternit jamais comme l’argent ou le fer, mais les scientifiques ne comprenaient pas complètement pourquoi il garde si bien son éclat. Cela a limité l’utilisation de l’or, surtout en chimie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université Tulane ont étudié comment les atomes à la surface de l’or se réarrangent quand on le coupe. Ils ont découvert que les atomes d’or forment un motif hexagonal à la surface, ce qui rend très difficile la réaction avec l’oxygène et empêche le ternissement. Grâce à des superordinateurs, ils ont montré que ce motif demande beaucoup d’énergie pour changer, ce qui garde l’or brillant la plupart du temps.

**Impact** – Cette découverte explique un mystère ancien sur la brillance de l’or en reliant cela à la forme des atomes à sa surface. Elle ouvre aussi de nouvelles possibilités pour modifier la surface de l’or afin qu’il réagisse davantage, ce qui pourrait aider les scientifiques à utiliser l’or comme catalyseur pour accélérer des réactions chimiques. Cela pourrait influencer beaucoup de domaines, comme l’électronique ou les procédés industriels.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent tester comment d’autres molécules réagissent avec l’or et étudier les alliages d’or, des mélanges avec d’autres métaux. Ils veulent aussi explorer des moyens de contrôler ce motif en utilisant l’électricité, ce qui pourrait permettre de nouvelles utilisations de l’or.

**Une phrase clé** – Les scientifiques ont découvert que l’éclat de l’or vient d’un motif atomique spécial à sa surface, et que changer ce motif pourrait rendre l’or utile pour de nouvelles réactions chimiques.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren por qué el oro permanece brillante y cómo cambiarlo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El oro es un metal que nunca se empaña como la plata o el hierro, pero los científicos no entendían completamente por qué mantiene su brillo. Esto ha limitado el uso del oro, especialmente en química.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad Tulane estudiaron cómo los átomos en la superficie del oro se reorganizan cuando se corta. Descubrieron que los átomos forman un patrón hexagonal en la superficie, lo que hace muy difícil que el oxígeno reaccione y cause empañamiento. Usando supercomputadoras, mostraron que este patrón necesita mucha energía para cambiar, lo que mantiene el oro brillante la mayoría del tiempo.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento explica un misterio antiguo sobre el brillo del oro al relacionarlo con la forma de los átomos en su superficie. También abre nuevas posibilidades para cambiar la superficie del oro para que reaccione más, lo que podría ayudar a usar el oro como catalizador para acelerar reacciones químicas. Esto podría afectar muchas áreas, desde mejorar la electrónica hasta procesos industriales.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren probar cómo reaccionan otras moléculas con el oro y estudiar aleaciones de oro, mezclas con otros metales. También quieren explorar maneras de controlar el patrón usando electricidad, lo que podría permitir nuevos usos del oro.

**Una frase clave** – Los científicos descubrieron que el brillo del oro viene de un patrón especial de átomos en su superficie, y cambiar este patrón podría hacer que el oro sea útil para nuevas reacciones químicas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a gleaming gold surface composed of tightly arranged hexagonal patterns symbolizing atomic reconstruction, with subtle hints of delicate oxygen molecules gently interacting but unable to penetrate or dull the gold’s shine, all rendered in soft natural golds, muted blues, and earthy browns.
